purpose: Determines the structural correlation sign between stocks and bonds based on the relative variance of demand versus supply shocks filtered through the central bank reaction function
components: New Keynesian three-equation model (IS, Phillips Curve, Taylor Rule)
Demand shock channel: growth fears → rate cuts → countercyclical bonds → negative correlation
Supply shock channel: cost-push inflation → discount rate repricing → positive correlation
Shock mix estimation: currently ~55-60% supply-driven (down from ~75% in 2022)
Central bank reaction function determines whether supply shocks are accommodated or offset
current_assessment: Current shock mix is roughly balanced with slight supply-side tilt, producing oscillating correlation. The framework correctly predicted the 2022 positive correlation episode and the subsequent partial normalization
limitations: Difficult to decompose shock mix in real time
Assumes the central bank reaction function is observable and stable
Does not account for fiscal channel separately from monetary channel
Binary demand/supply classification may miss hybrid shocks

purpose: Maps the central bank inflation coefficient (φ_π) to correlation regime outcomes, providing a single institutional variable that determines regime stability
components: φ_π < 1.0: indeterminacy zone, generates persistent positive correlation
φ_π 1.0-1.5: danger/transition zone with unstable correlation
φ_π > 1.5: determinacy zone, generates negative correlation
Revealed preference estimation from policy actions (hiking cycles, easing phases)
Current estimate: φ_π ≈ 1.3-1.7 based on easing phase behavior
current_assessment: The Fed demonstrated φ_π ≈ 1.8-2.0 during the 2022-23 hiking cycle but the current easing phase suggests φ_π ≈ 1.3-1.7, placing the regime in the transition zone. The aggressive tightening against political pressure is strong evidence of intact credibility
limitations: φ_π is not directly observable and must be inferred from policy actions
Estimates are retrospective — the current easing phase estimate is preliminary
Does not account for balance sheet policy (QE/QT) as a separate instrument
Assumes a single reaction function rather than state-dependent responses

purpose: Provides a structured analogue framework for how positive-to-negative correlation regime transitions resolve, based on the 1994-98 experience
components: Phase 1: Term premium shock (1994 bond massacre) — PRESENT TODAY
Phase 2: Fiscal consolidation credibility — UNAVAILABLE at 6-7% GDP deficits
Phase 3: Exogenous EM crisis providing catalyst — possible but not predictable
Phase 4: LTCM/safe-haven validation demonstrating bond hedge — requires Phase 3
Phase 5: Productivity boom (1990s tech) — AI potential but requires Phase 2 context
current_assessment: Today has completed Phase 1 only. Phase 2 is unavailable absent fiscal consolidation, which has no political pathway through 2028. Without Phase 2, subsequent phases cannot replicate their 1990s effects. The oscillating correlation environment is likely to persist
limitations: Single historical sequence (N=1) as the template
Phases may not need to occur in exact order
An alternative resolution path not captured by this sequence may exist
AI productivity boom may not require fiscal consolidation as a prerequisite

purpose: Classifies the possible resolution paths for the maturity-dependent correlation bifurcation based on international case studies
components: Path A - Fiscal consolidation (~10%): US 1994-98, Canada 1995-97
Path B - Central bank backstop (~30%): ECB OMT 2012, BoE Sept 2022, Fed March 2020
Path C - Inflation de-anchoring (~15%): Brazil 2015-16, Turkey 2018-19
Path D - Managed financial repression (~30%): Japan 2003 VaR shock → YCC, Italy 2012 pre-OMT
Remaining ~15% for alternative or hybrid paths
current_assessment: Today's trajectory most closely maps to Paths B or D (~60% combined), both involving central bank accommodation of fiscal dynamics. Consistent with fiscal dominance findings. Path A is politically unavailable; Path C is a tail risk
limitations: Probability assignments are subjective
International cases may not transfer to US institutional context
Paths may combine rather than being discrete
Timeline for resolution is unspecified

purpose: Identifies the three conditions that must be simultaneously present for a durable correlation regime shift, explaining why the 2022-25 episode produced oscillation rather than entrenchment
components: Factor 1: Inflation expectations persistently above target (partially present at 2.2-2.5% breakevens)
Factor 2: Term premium sustained above threshold (partially present)
Factor 3: Dollar breakdown / structural weakening (absent — DXY 100-107)
All three must be simultaneously present for durable positive correlation
Missing any one factor produces oscillation rather than entrenchment
current_assessment: Two of three factors are partially present; the dollar factor is absent. This correctly predicts the observed oscillating behavior since 2022 and suggests entrenchment requires dollar weakness
limitations: N=4 regime transitions as empirical base — small sample
Dollar dynamics are endogenous to the other two factors
Threshold levels for each factor are not precisely defined
Model may miss structural breaks that change the conjunction requirements

purpose: Assesses the state of monetary policy transmission through each individual channel to determine the overall degree of transmission impairment under positive correlation
components: Interest rate channel: near-full functionality (rate-sensitive sectors respond)
Credit channel: moderately impaired by $1.5T+ private credit substitution bypassing bank lending
Expectations channel: degraded but not broken (forward guidance attenuated 30-50%)
Asset price channel: partially offset (bond and equity channels can work against each other under positive correlation)
Overall assessment: 'partially functional' — attenuation, not collapse
current_assessment: Transmission is impaired but functional. The interest rate channel works, the credit channel leaks through private credit, forward guidance is attenuated 30-50%, and the asset price channel partially self-cancels. The system works well enough to prevent the correlation regime from locking positive but not well enough to drive it clearly negative
limitations: Channel assessments are qualitative rather than precisely quantified
Channels interact in complex ways not captured by individual assessment
Private credit channel leakage is a new structural feature with limited data
Relative importance of channels may shift across the cycle

purpose: Provides historical base rates for correlation regime persistence conditioned on the fiscal environment, refining unconditional base rates
components: Unconditional base rate: 25-35% of zero-crossings become persistent regime shifts
Conditional on moderate declining inflation + fiscal deficits below 5% GDP: 75-80% revert to negative (4/4 episodes)
Conditional on moderate declining inflation + fiscal deficits above 5% GDP: n=2, ambiguous results
Current configuration: moderate declining inflation + 6-7% deficit falls in uncertain zone
Bayesian updating framework for incorporating new data
current_assessment: The unconditional base rate (25-35%) is a useful anchor that consensus has ignored, but conditioning on the fiscal variable reduces its informativeness. Today's 6-7% deficit places us in genuinely uncertain territory with insufficient historical analogues
limitations: Treats all zero-crossings as drawn from the same distribution
Small sample sizes especially in the high-deficit conditional
Conditioning on fiscal deficits may miss other important variables
Base rates assume stationarity of the underlying process

purpose: Classifies recessions by trigger type to predict the correlation sign during economic downturns
components: Fed-triggered recessions (1970, 1980, 1982): 3/3 immediate negative correlation
Exogenous-shock recessions (1974, 1990, 2001, 2008, 2020): 2/5 initial positive correlation
Overall: 7/8 recessions eventually produce negative correlation
Fed rate-cutting response is the most powerful negative-correlation mechanism
Time to negative assertion: immediate for Fed-triggered, 1-3 months for exogenous-shock
current_assessment: The 7/8 overall hit rate is strong, supporting maintenance of some duration exposure even under positive correlation. The Fed's historical willingness to cut aggressively in recessions (2001: cut despite 2.6% core CPI; 2020: cut to zero) suggests this pattern will repeat
limitations: Recession trigger type is rarely identifiable in advance
Sample size is small (8 recessions)
Future recessions may have novel trigger types (e.g., AI-driven)
The 1/8 exception (likely 1974) may be more relevant if inflation is elevated
